cause_stage: As a senior researcher who has published extensively over the past five years, and with considerable experience in writing and publishing academic articles, the most intense feeling I experience is pressure, but also pleasure, contentment, and relief when the paper is published. These feelings primarily stem from the continuous expectations—both internal and external—to maintain a high level of productivity and scholarly contribution. Balancing ongoing research projects, publication deadlines, and the need to uphold the quality of my work often creates a sustained sense of pressure, which I identified as the dominant emotion in this context.
cause_general: Overall, I would rate my feelings about my life as a researcher as very positive, despite the challenges and pressures that come with the role. I find the work intellectually fulfilling, and I value the contributions I am able to make through my publications and ongoing projects. The main cause of these positive feelings is the sense of purpose and accomplishment that comes from conducting meaningful research and successfully disseminating my findings. As a senior researcher with substantial publishing experience over the past five years, I feel motivated by the impact of my work, the opportunities for continuous learning, and the recognition that comes with scholarly productivity. Even though the pressure to maintain a high level of output can be intense at times, the overall satisfaction and intellectual engagement outweigh the difficulties.

cause_stage: Stress

Analyzing my data was very time-consuming and demanding. At times, the workload led to feelings of fatigue, discouragement, sadness, and even disgust, along with a fear of not achieving the results I was hoping for.
When the results finally came and turned out to be very satisfying, everything changed. The fatigue and negative feelings faded away, replaced by renewed energy and motivation. I then wrote my report with enthusiasm, as my passion came back, as if I had fully recharged my batteries and found new meaning in my work.
cause_general: My experience as a researcher has been very positive overall, especially since I can now clearly see the results of my work. Being able to maintain a balance between my research activities, my commitment to my students, and my family life feels like a real achievement to me.  Involving my residents in my research projects, supporting them, and helping to train future researchers is an essential part of my professional journey and a major source of both personal and academic satisfaction.

cause_stage: Data collection is (in my opinion) one of the most interesting stage of a research project since the technical aspects have more or less already been validated and the project reaches a stage where it yields answers to the questions that were initially defined. This is an intellectually stimulating phase where we alternate between experiments, data analysis and brainstorming, to validate or invalidate initial hypotheses, generate new hypotheses and design new experiments to address them. It is also a stage in which opposite emotions, such as pleasure, amusement and fear, can alternate as positive or negative results are collected.
cause_general: Our work leads us to alternate between joy, fear, sadness and anger as we obtain (or not) funding to carry out our projects, we obtain positive or negative results, we discover flaws in our experimental designs or simply external problems that can negatively affect the outcome of our experiments. We are in constant competition with other scientists which can also have a negative impact on our feelings. However, it is also a very rewarding job when we are able to collect good results, and finis a project.

cause_general: I selected “neutral” because my overall feelings about my life as a researcher oscillate between extremely positive and extremely negative periods. On the positive side, I genuinely enjoy my work: I find research intellectually stimulating and meaningful, I appreciate several of my colleagues, and I value my relationship with students, which I find motivating and rewarding. On the negative side, I experience significant frustration due to administrative burdens and the difficulty of securing funding for my research. cause_stage:
Interest: When writing your scientific research down, you move beyond analysis and try to synthesise meaning. It is interesting to see how your findings differ from others or generate same insights and motivate how they can impact your field of research. 

At the same time it also comes with disappointment. Where you have to make do with what you have, but it feels never good enough. You have to look at your research and find its flaws, also feeling like you should have done things differently from the beginning because it would have saved you so much time/effort or it would have provided different insights perhaps. 

And then there is the fear that no one will be interested in actually publishing/reading your research. You wonder why you've spend so much effort on it anyways. 

It is an emotional rollercoaster.
cause_general: It is nice to work on novel things. Life as an healthcare research provides me with a lot of flexibility which in an engineering company I would not necessarily have. I sometimes miss adding any practical value and despise how long everything takes. Pursuing an career in research within academia I do not find attractive because it is also a life with a lot of uncertainties (financially, where you are positioned, durations of contracts).

cause_stage: Because the research projects that I thought would be useful were not accepted.
cause_general: I experience fluctuating emotions. Sometimes, especially when I submit a research paper to a journal, I feel hopeful, but then the process takes too long, and I lose enthusiasm. Sometimes, revisions come in, I make the peer review corrections, and I think it will be accepted, and I become hopeful again. However, after the revisions, the paper is suddenly rejected. Despair overwhelms me. Over time, this feeling disappears, and I submit the paper to a new journal. The process continues in this vicious cycle.

cause_stage: The most intense feeling during this stage was strong interest, arising from developing the research idea and shaping it into a viable project with clear questions and methods. This phase was intellectually engaging and motivating. Alongside this, I experienced frustration and low-level anger linked to structural aspects of the research funding system, particularly the low proportion of projects that ultimately receive funding. This generated a sense of tension between the effort and creativity invested in project development and the limited likelihood of success, though these feelings were secondary to overall engagement with the research process.
